## 6) Which forecasting methods are worth using

### Unified comparison table: forecasting and decision methods

| Method | Data requirement | Interpretability | Operational complexity | Robustness to holidays/regime shifts | Where it usually wins | Where it usually fails |
|---|---|---|---|---|---|---|
| **Static schedule / seasonal naive** | Very low | Very high | Very low | Strong for known calendars; weak for unusual days | Clock-like peaks, known business calendars [[234]](https://openforecast.org/adam/simpleForecastingMethods.html)(https://openforecast.org/adam/simpleForecastingMethods.html) [[209]](https://superorbital.io/blog/scaling-smarter-instant-nodes-zero-wait/)(https://superorbital.io/blog/scaling-smarter-instant-nodes-zero-wait/) | Novel events, drifting patterns |
| **Moving average / simple heuristics** | Low | High | Low | Better than static schedules for short-memory noise; poor on long seasonal effects | Mildly noisy repeated traffic [[234]](https://openforecast.org/adam/simpleForecastingMethods.html)(https://openforecast.org/adam/simpleForecastingMethods.html) | Sudden spikes, holiday effects |
| **ARIMA** | Moderate | Medium | Medium | Reasonable classical baseline; weaker on nonlinear patterns | Statistical baseline, stable series [[241]](https://otexts.com/fpp3/missing-outliers.html)(https://otexts.com/fpp3/missing-outliers.html) [[242]](https://www.mdpi.com/2078-2489/15/4/222)(https://www.mdpi.com/2078-2489/15/4/222) | Sparse, noisy, nonlinear workloads |
| **Prophet** | Moderate with calendar features | High | Medium | Strong when daily/weekly/holiday structure exists [[233]](https://facebook.github.io/prophet/static/prophet_paper_20170113.pdf)(https://facebook.github.io/prophet/static/prophet_paper_20170113.pdf) [[239]](http://facebook.github.io/prophet/docs/seasonality,_holiday_effects,_and_regressors.html)(http://facebook.github.io/prophet/docs/seasonality,_holiday_effects,_and_regressors.html) | Business-calendar-aware services | Weak history, rapid regime shifts |
| **LSTM / Bi-LSTM** | High | Low | High | Can model nonlinear residuals, but harder to govern [[243]](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/9/3835)(https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/9/3835) | Large, stable, complex datasets | Limited data, explainability requirements |
| **Prophet + LSTM hybrid** | High | Low-to-medium | Highest | Promising for strong seasonality plus nonlinear residuals [[114]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1509165/full)(https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1509165/full) | Mature ML operations with clear seasonal structure | Most teams; overkill for basic patterns |
| **Recommended when...** |  |  |  |  |  | **Use schedules first, Prophet second, ARIMA as a baseline, and deep models only when backtesting shows material gains over simpler baselines.** |

### 6.1 The ranking that holds up in production

For most teams, the practical order of preference is still:

1. **Schedules / schedule-plus-buffer**
2. **Simple heuristics / seasonal naive**
3. **Prophet**
4. **ARIMA**
5. **LSTM / Bi-LSTM / hybrids only with evidence** [[234]](https://openforecast.org/adam/simpleForecastingMethods.html)(https://openforecast.org/adam/simpleForecastingMethods.html) [[235]](https://statmodeling.stat.columbia.edu/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/M5_accuracy_competition.pdf)(https://statmodeling.stat.columbia.edu/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/M5_accuracy_competition.pdf) [[233]](https://facebook.github.io/prophet/static/prophet_paper_20170113.pdf)(https://facebook.github.io/prophet/static/prophet_paper_20170113.pdf) [[243]](https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/9/3835)(https://www.mdpi.com/2076-3417/11/9/3835) [[114]](https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1509165/full)(https://www.frontiersin.org/journals/computer-science/articles/10.3389/fcomp.2025.1509165/full)

The reason is not anti-ML conservatism. It is that node scaling is a **closed-loop operational system**, not a Kaggle competition. The best model is the one that makes the right capacity available early enough, fails safely when wrong, and can be debugged by operators at 03:00.

### 6.2 Why simple methods often win

The M5 competition results are important because they quantify how often sophisticated models fail to outperform simple baselines: only **48.4%** beat naive, only **35.8%** beat seasonal naive, and only **7.5%** beat the strongest simple benchmark using final submissions [[235]](https://statmodeling.stat.columbia.edu/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/M5_accuracy_competition.pdf)(https://statmodeling.stat.columbia.edu/wp-content/uploads/2021/10/M5_accuracy_competition.pdf). That does not mean deep models are useless. It means the burden of proof is on the more complex method.

For proactive node scaling, “good enough” usually means:
- it gets the baseline in place before demand,
- it does not oscillate,
- it does not require weekly heroics,
- it beats the current schedule on either cost or missed peak latency.

### 6.3 Which error metrics matter

The report correctly warns against relying on **MAPE alone**, especially for zero-heavy or sparse series [[246]](https://robjhyndman.com/papers/foresight.pdf)(https://robjhyndman.com/papers/foresight.pdf) [[247]](https://robjhyndman.com/hyndsight/wape.html)(https://robjhyndman.com/hyndsight/wape.html). For autoscaling, model evaluation should include both forecast metrics and operational outcome metrics:

**Forecast quality**
- MASE or RMSSE for scale-normalized accuracy,
- directional accuracy around the peak window,
- peak-timing error, not just average error.

**Scaling outcome quality**
- how many minutes early was capacity ready?
- how many user-facing requests hit throttled or degraded service?
- how many excess node-minutes were paid for?
- how many unnecessary scale-in/scale-out cycles occurred?
- did SLOs hold?

This is why production predictive systems often gate forecasts by numerical quality **and** retain reactive fallbacks [[94]](https://kedify.io/resources/blog/predictive-autoscaling/)(https://kedify.io/resources/blog/predictive-autoscaling/) [[54]](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-09-predictive-autoscaling-ml-models/view)(https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-09-predictive-autoscaling-ml-models/view).

## 7) Platform-specific implementation patterns

A cross-provider finding matters here: **core managed Kubernetes autoscaling features are generally reactive, and none of the reviewed provider-native node autoscaler features is documented as a first-class cron-style “scheduled node pool autoscaler” product** [[188]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/APIReference/API_NodegroupScalingConfig.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/APIReference/API_NodegroupScalingConfig.html). Time-based node scaling usually means provider API automation or external orchestration.

## Amazon EKS

EKS has the broadest menu:
- **Managed node groups + CA** [[36]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/best-practices/cas.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/best-practices/cas.html) [[69]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/managed-node-groups.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/managed-node-groups.html)
- **open-source Karpenter** [[74]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/autoscaling.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/autoscaling.html) [[73]](https://karpenter.sh/)(https://karpenter.sh/)
- **EKS Auto Mode** [[74]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/autoscaling.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/autoscaling.html) [[189]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/automode.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/automode.html)
- **warm pools for managed node groups**, added in **April 2026** [[14]](https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2026/04/amazon-eks-managed-node-groups-ec2-warm-pools/)(https://aws.amazon.com/about-aws/whats-new/2026/04/amazon-eks-managed-node-groups-ec2-warm-pools/) [[53]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/warm-pools-managed-node-groups.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/warm-pools-managed-node-groups.html)
- **ODCR / Capacity Blocks / reservations** for capacity assurance [[136]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/best-practices/aiml-compute.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/best-practices/aiml-compute.html) [[137]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/capacity-blocks-mng.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/capacity-blocks-mng.html) [[194]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/auto-odcr.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/auto-odcr.html)

Two implementation cautions are especially important on EKS:

1. `NodegroupScalingConfig.desiredSize` is a direct control surface for proactive scaling, but AWS explicitly warns that if you use CA, changing `desiredSize` directly can conflict with CA [[188]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/APIReference/API_NodegroupScalingConfig.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/APIReference/API_NodegroupScalingConfig.html).  
   That means scheduled node-group scaling is viable, but only if you define the control relationship clearly:
   - either CA is the main controller and schedules adjust guardrails carefully,
   - or external automation is authoritative and CA is limited or excluded for that pool.

2. EKS managed node groups are backed by Auto Scaling groups whose live values can diverge from stored config and later resync through EKS workflows [[69]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/managed-node-groups.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/eks/latest/userguide/managed-node-groups.html).  
   So if you use scheduled API mutations, the operational source of truth must be explicit.

**Best EKS pattern by scenario**
- fixed groups, predictable peaks: scheduled node-group scaling + CA/HPA + small buffer;
- flexible compute: Karpenter or EKS Auto Mode + schedule/predictive floor + optional static/warm pool;
- guaranteed capacity: reservations or capacity blocks + scheduled floor + cautious reactive correction.

## Azure AKS

AKS offers:
- manual or CA-managed node-pool scaling [[138]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/concepts-scale)(https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/concepts-scale) [[106]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/scale-cluster)(https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/scale-cluster),
- **Node Auto Provisioning (NAP)** as managed Karpenter-like dynamic provisioning [[190]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/node-auto-provisioning)(https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/node-auto-provisioning) [[141]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/node-auto-provisioning-aksnodeclass)(https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/node-auto-provisioning-aksnodeclass),
- **scale-to-zero** for User pools under defined caveats [[106]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/scale-cluster)(https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/scale-cluster),
- capacity reservation constructs for assured pools [[144]](https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/best-practices-cost)(https://learn.microsoft.com/en-us/azure/aks/best-practices-cost).

The AKS-specific implication is similar to EKS: if the pool itself is constrained, proactive node-count changes come from **external orchestration**, not from CA. If the environment is flexible, NAP becomes the better node realization layer.

## Google GKE

GKE offers:
- node-pool **cluster autoscaling** [[15]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-autoscaler)(https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-autoscaler),
- **node auto-provisioning / auto-creation** [[145]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-auto-provisioning)(https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-auto-provisioning),
- **Autopilot** for fully managed compute provisioning [[15]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-autoscaler)(https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/cluster-autoscaler) [[83]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/cluster-autoscaler)(https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/how-to/cluster-autoscaler),
- **ComputeClasses** for shaping preferred machine families, Spot, reservations, and policy [[146]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/about-custom-compute-classes)(https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/about-custom-compute-classes) [[145]](https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-auto-provisioning)(https://docs.cloud.google.com/kubernetes-engine/docs/concepts/node-auto-provisioning).

GKE’s strength is not first-class scheduled node scaling; it is flexible policy-based provisioning. So the proactive pattern is usually:
- use workload-side schedules or prediction,
- use GKE cluster autoscaling or node auto-provisioning to realize capacity,
- reduce cold-start pain with image streaming or active buffer capacity [[221]](https://www.pluralsight.com/resources/blog/cloud/gke-ludicrous-speed-gke-image-streaming-speeds-up-container-starts)(https://www.pluralsight.com/resources/blog/cloud/gke-ludicrous-speed-gke-image-streaming-speeds-up-container-starts) [[219]](https://www.doit.com/blog/instant-on-scaling-eliminating-node-provisioning-delays-in-gke-with-active-buffer)(https://www.doit.com/blog/instant-on-scaling-eliminating-node-provisioning-delays-in-gke-with-active-buffer).

## Oracle OKE / OCI

OCI historically emphasized managed node pools with Cluster Autoscaler, but the major update is the **GA release of Karpenter Provider for OCI in April 2026** [[191]](https://docs.oracle.com/en-us/iaas/Content/ContEng/Tasks/contengautoscalingclusters.htm)(https://docs.oracle.com/en-us/iaas/Content/ContEng/Tasks/contengautoscalingclusters.htm) [[86]](https://blogs.oracle.com/cloud-infrastructure/smarter-kubernetes-autoscaling-with-karpenter)(https://blogs.oracle.com/cloud-infrastructure/smarter-kubernetes-autoscaling-with-karpenter) [[151]](https://docs.oracle.com/iaas/Content/ContEng/Tasks/conteng-kpo.htm)(https://docs.oracle.com/iaas/Content/ContEng/Tasks/conteng-kpo.htm). That is significant because it materially improves proactive/flexible node design for OCI users who previously had to live inside fixed pool shapes.

## Self-managed Kubernetes on VMs

This is where custom automation is most justified and most dangerous. Typical patterns are:
- CA if a supported cloudprovider integration exists [[79]](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler)(https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler) [[152]](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/oci/README.md)(https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/oci/README.md) [[156]](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/azure/README.md)(https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/azure/README.md),
- external schedulers/CronJobs for proactive pool changes [[89]](https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-09-scheduled-node-pool-scaling/view)(https://oneuptime.com/blog/post/2026-02-09-scheduled-node-pool-scaling/view) [[156]](https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/azure/README.md)(https://github.com/kubernetes/autoscaler/blob/master/cluster-autoscaler/cloudprovider/azure/README.md),
- overprovisioning where no managed warm-pool service exists [[37]](https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/node-overprovisioning/)(https://kubernetes.io/docs/tasks/administer-cluster/node-overprovisioning/) [[91]](https://github.com/redhat-cop/proactive-node-scaling-operator)(https://github.com/redhat-cop/proactive-node-scaling-operator).

Because the platform itself offers fewer safety rails, the rollout discipline must be stronger: version control, approvals, dry runs, and production-like game day validation.

## 8) Operational best practices, testing, and rollout safety

## 8.1 Fix pod requests before scaling logic

This deserves stronger emphasis than it usually gets. Node autoscaling is **request-based** [[2]](https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/node-autoscaling/)(https://kubernetes.io/docs/concepts/cluster-administration/node-autoscaling/). If requests are inflated, the autoscaler buys too many nodes. If requests are understated, the cluster packs too tightly and real runtime contention increases. A proactive system built on bad requests just makes the wrong decision earlier.

## 8.2 Validate against concrete SLOs

A useful production KPI exists here. AWS EKS scalability guidance cites the community pod startup latency SLO: the **99th percentile per cluster-day of schedulable stateless pod startup latency**, excluding image pulls and init containers, should be **≤ 5 seconds** [[164]](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/deep-dive-into-amazon-eks-scalability-testing/)(https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/containers/deep-dive-into-amazon-eks-scalability-testing/). That is a better rollout target than vague “faster startup” claims.

Operationally, separate:
- scheduling/capacity delay,
- kubelet start delay,
- image pull delay,
- app readiness delay.

If pod startup SLI looks good but end-user latency is still bad, the problem is often upstream at node acquisition or image pull. If node counts are correct but pods do not become Ready fast enough, the proactive logic is not the bottleneck.

## 8.3 Shadow-mode backtesting before mutation

For predictive logic, run **forecast-only / shadow mode** first [[254]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/predictive-scaling-policy-overview.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/predictive-scaling-policy-overview.html) [[255]](https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/predictive-scaling-graphs.html)(https://docs.aws.amazon.com/autoscaling/ec2/userguide/predictive-scaling-graphs.html) [[257]](https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/evaluating-predictive-scaling-for-amazon-ec2-capacity-optimization/)(https://aws.amazon.com/blogs/compute/evaluating-predictive-scaling-for-amazon-ec2-capacity-optimization/). Compare:
- forecast vs actual leading metric,
- forecast-derived capacity vs actual required capacity,
- missed-peak minutes,
- idle node-minutes,
- scale-in churn.

Do not promote a predictive controller because the model “looks accurate.” Promote it only if it **beats the current schedule-plus-buffer baseline** on operational outcomes.

## 8.4 Structured load tests

Use realistic ramps and falloffs, not only flat load [[252]](https://aws.amazon.com/solutions/guidance/kubernetes-based-game-load-testing-on-aws/)(https://aws.amazon.com/solutions/guidance/kubernetes-based-game-load-testing-on-aws/) [[253]](https://github.com/aws-solutions-library-samples/guidance-for-kubernetes-based-game-load-testing-on-aws)(https://github.com/aws-solutions-library-samples/guidance-for-kubernetes-based-game-load-testing-on-aws). A useful test suite includes:
- pre-peak ramp,
- sudden burst,
- sustained peak,
- oscillating mid-peak plateau,
- post-peak decay.

That sequence tests the whole chain:
**signal → replica increase → node acquisition → readiness → scale-in**.

## 8.5 Game days and operational controls

AWS’s game day guidance is relevant because proactive scaling changes both automation and operator response. Run regular game days in production-like environments, with clear environment markers, approvals, and execution safeguards so destructive scripts cannot hit production accidentally [[250]](https://wa.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/2020-07-02T19-33-23/wat.concept.gameday.en.html)(https://wa.aws.amazon.com/wellarchitected/2020-07-02T19-33-23/wat.concept
